channel.title: Computer Support in Mildenhall
channel.description: Recent content on Computer Support in Mildenhall

item.title: About Me
item.description:
A brief career summary

Time served

I’ve been a computer professional since 1981 and have a wide range of experience in the computer industry, particularly in real-time software engineering (the complicated can’t-afford-to-get-it-wrong kind of computing).

Posts held

My first job was working for a government research organisation, and I then moved into the private sector to work for a large telecommunications equipment manufacturer. After that I became a contractor and worked on printed circuit board design software, a trans-Atlantic optical fibre cable project, consultancy and customer support for a major semiconductor manufacturer, network management software for the UK’s main supplier of landline-based telephone services, and software to store large maps in a geographical information system. I’m now self-employed and work from home. You can see my work history in detail by viewing my LinkedIn profile.

Professional interests

Unusually for a person with an engineering and programming background, I enjoy technical and other writing, and am interested in typography, eye-catching document layout, and graphic design in general. I particularly like creating 3D graphics (stills rather than animations).

item.title: Advice
item.description:
Advice

Helping people to avoid ‘lemons’ and choose the best new equipment such as:

  • computers and laptops
  • mice and other pointing devices
  • inkjet and laser printers
  • cables
  • memory
  • external hard drives
  • USB memory sticks
  • routers and range extenders

Helping people to side-step expensive or problematic products and choose the best software such as:

  • operating systems
  • office packages
  • high-quality typefaces
  • Internet browsers
  • image viewers
  • e-mail programs
  • anti-virus packages

Helping people to steer clear of mediocre or poor providers and find the best services such as:

  • landline-based broadband
  • mobile broadband
  • cloud storage
  • e-mail providers

item.title: Contact Me
item.description:
How to get in touch

E-mail or telephone

To ask about computer problems or lessons, e-mail john.geoffrey.wallis@outlook.com, or call me on 07748 560851 for a quicker response (I’m not one of those people who checks my e-mail every five minutes).

Social and business networks

I have Facebook, LinkedIn, Streetlife, and Twitter accounts, although I don’t use LinkedIn much and have never sent a single ‘tweet’. My personal Facebook page is here and I also have a ‘Computer Support in Mildenhall’ Facebook page here. My LinkedIn page is here and my Streetlife page is here.

Technical co-operation

I also have a Github account (Github is a place where software developers get involved in collaborative projects). This website is actually hosted on Github. I know the name is unappealing, but a lot of people in the computer industry set up facilities with off-putting names, sometimes on purpose. I would move my website to somewhere with a better name, but Google would probably lose track of me and then it would take a lot of work and a long time to get found again.

Real-life location

Despite all this phone and Internet business, I do actually exist somewhere, and still use ordinary ‘snail mail’ for quite a few things. My postal address is:

John Wallis
18 Bury Road
Mildenhall
Bury St. Edmunds
Suffolk
IP28 7HT

item.title: Credits
item.description:
Credits and legal information

Site copyright

This website © 2016 J. G. Wallis. All right reserved.

Fonts

The fonts used on this site are Titillium Web © 2009-2011 Accademia di Belle Arti di Urbino and students of the MA course in Visual Design, Open Sans © 2010-2011 Google Corporation, and Font Awesome 4.2.0 © 2014 Dave Gandy.

The Titillium Web and Font Awesome fonts are available under SIL’s Open Font licence. The Open Sans font is available under version 2.0 of the Apache Licence.

The basic CSS code to use Font Awesome is licensed under the MIT licence.

How the site was built

There’s no complicated content management system behind this site, so it should load quickly and be very secure. It was built ‘statically’ using Sassc, Bourbon, Neat, Bitters, Normalize, and Hugo. You probably don’t care about any of these technicalities, but if you’d like help creating a site of your own then please feel free to get in touch.

Software used to build the site

The versions of the various different bits of software used to build this site are:

  • Sassc based on Sass version 3.4 (Sassc licence)
  • Bourbon version 4.2.7 (MIT licence)
  • Neat version 1.8.0 (MIT licence)
  • Bitters version 1.3.1 (MIT licence)
  • Normalize version 3.0.3 (MIT licence)
  • Hugo version 0.16 (Apache 2.0 licence)

No cookies

This site doesn’t use cookies, collect information about you, or track your activities in any way. There’s no need for any ‘I understand and agree’ nonsense, so that makes a nice change, doesn’t it?

item.title: Internet
item.description:
Internet

Helping people to make the most of the Internet by:

  • setting up e-mail accounts
  • building and maintaining simple Web sites
  • setting up Web hosting accounts
  • helping to register Web site names
  • explaining Internet technicalities
  • demonstrating useful and interesting Web sites such as…
    • Google
    • BBC iPlayer
    • WikiPedia

item.title: Maintenance
item.description:
Maintenance

Cleaning, speeding up, and upgrading computers and laptops. Sorting out almost all hardware and software tasks, such as:

  • cleaning the dust out of computers and laptops, which can cause overheating and excessive fan noise
  • removing unnecessary files that gradually accumulate and clog up the system
  • deleting useless entries from the Windows registry, which is a database holding details of the entire system set-up
  • switching off programs that constantly run in the background, but are not needed in your particular case
  • killing adware, and so-called ‘search assistants’ that hijack your Internet searches and send you off to places you don’t really want to visit
  • stopping ‘junkware’ from starting up every time you start Windows (this is often installed by computer and printer manufacturers)
  • backing up data onto external hard disk drives, DVDs, or USB memory sticks
  • fitting new cartridges in inkjet and laser printers
  • checking the Windows file system for errors, and fixing any that are found
  • diagnosing possible hard drive problems and looking for early signs of failure (modern hard disk drives usually record their own performance and error statistics)
  • upgrading hard disk drives to solid-state drives
  • reading the internal temperatures of various parts of the system to see if any of them are running too hot
  • making sure the system regularly creates ‘system restore’ points that allow you to roll back to previous times, just in case the system starts misbehaving

item.title: Oh Dear!
item.description:
Modern ‘customer service’

Don’t waste your time

Hanging on the telephone

These days, computer and related equipment manufacturers, and ancillary service providers, only offer customer support through call centre hotlines and online chat facilities. They don’t want to deal with letters or e-mails, and they often don’t have enough people manning the telephones and chatlines, so customers end up waiting in long queues.

Talking to a robot

In an attempt to shorten chatline queues, while saving money on staff, a lot of providers have even introduced robotic chatline operators that try to look like humans, but are actually just machines. They might work for simple queries, but not for anything complicated. If you end up talking to one of these things and it can’t solve your problem, you might get put through to a real person, but it’s another layer of nonsense that you have to get through.

Jumping through hoops

One size fits all

For a complex issue, it is probably better to telephone, but then you’ll run into several problems. First you’ll get the ‘press “1” for this and press “2” for that’ problem, which is enough to test anybody’s nerves. Then you‘ll get the ‘your call is important to us’ routine. When you eventually get to speak to someone, they’ll ask you to go through various standard procedures that they always suggest, many of which are time-consuming and have virtually no chance of being the solution to your problem.

The same answers for every problem

Have you done a ‘clean boot’? Have you checked the hard disk drive for errors? Have you tried backing up all your data then reinstalling the operating system? You have to play along with these suggestions otherwise you are handing the support people the excuse they need to hang up, but a lot of their suggestions are either steps on the road to nowhere or ‘sledgehammer to crack a nut’ solutions.

You have to do the work

The reality is that these businesses are no longer interested in taking responsibility for problems, if they ever were, but want their customers to do most of the work themselves. This is not real customer service. If you take your car to a garage because the engine is making a strange noise, you don’t expect to be directed to an empty corner of the building and handed a tray of tools to sort out the problem yourself, but this is how most of the computer industry works. In fact the computer industry is worse because quite often, the tools they offer you don’t work and you just end up with ‘unable to solve problem’ messages.

Recognise the limitations of the system

You can get some basic advice over the telephone or by chatting online, if you are prepared to wait long enough, but most of the work is pushed onto the customer. Making customers jump through a lot of hoops, waste a lot of time, and take a lot of risks, is an odd way to operate, but unfortunately that’s how the computer industry works.

Computers are complicated

It’s not easy

I do have some sympathy for the manufacturers and service providers. Computers are very complex machines, even if they look simple on the outside, and there are so many ways of configuring them that problems can be hard to track down and eventually solve. Large organisations often make sure that all their computers are configured in exactly the same way for consistency and ease of maintenance, but in domestic environments computer set-ups are usually unique.

It’s not quick

Manufacturers and service providers cannot afford to spend very long on each problem that is reported to them because these puzzles are often difficult and numerous, and in any case, solving them results in little if any profit. However, that doesn’t do the customer much good.

The real answer

Find someone who’ll actually do the work

The solution for the customer who wants to get their problems solved quickly, and properly, is to hire a local expert who will sort everything out without any buck-passing. Engaging someone who will take responsibility for the problem means the customer can get on with their life and have peace of mind. So talk to a real computer person, not someone from a call centre who reads out lists of instructions from a script.

Look at reliable online reviews

Finding a reliable computer person isn’t hard. I often see people on Facebook and Streetlife asking for recommendations, but the easiest way to find a local expert is to search Google for something like ‘computer problem Mildenhall’ and see what pops up. You’ll find far more opinions by doing this, going back quite a long time, and they’ll be a lot more useful than the ‘I always go to Fred’ responses that you’re likely to get from social media.

Choose someone who ranks highly

In this particular case, my details will probably appear quite near the top of the first page. You’ll be able to see my reviews on FreeIndex, all of which are five-star reviews, and compare me with local competitors. It should then be easy to make up your mind who to contact.

item.title: Prices
item.description:
Good value and always reasonable

Current charges

For the year from 6th April 2016 to 5th April 2017, my basic rate for computer troubleshooting is £28 per hour with a minimum chargeable time of one hour. After the first hour, time is charged in quarter-hour chunks, with any part of a quarter-hour counting as a whole quarter-hour. So one-and-a-quarter hours of work is £35, one hour and twenty-five minutes is £42, and so on. This rate is very competitive, being considerably lower than many professionals would charge.

There’s no call-out fee for local work. but there’s a half-hour charge (£14) for any job that’s more than five miles from my home, as the crow flies. I have to charge this to account for the time it takes to drive both ways, fuel, business insurance, and sometimes parking as well. However, most computer support businesses always charge a call-out fee, and the only way you can avoid it is by taking your problem to them, which is far less inconvenient.

Charges for tuition are considerably lower. Please get in touch for further details.

No fix, no fee

If I’m called out to solve a specific problem and find that I can’t fix it, I don’t charge anything at all. If, however, I’m called out to solve a problem and am successful, but am then asked to look at another problem that I can’t solve, I still charge for fixing the initial problem.

‘No fix, no fee’ means that if I can’t solve a problem then I won’t charge. However, if a potential customer doesn’t give me a chance to fix whatever problem I’ve found then there’ll be a small diagnosis fee (equivalent to half-an-hour’s time). I had to introduce this policy shortly after starting my business because sometimes a potential customer would call me out to discover what was wrong with their computer, then decide to not bother fixing it, or to fix it themselves, or to get somebody else to fix it.

Being reasonable

If you call me out to do a job and it turns out to be very quick and simple, I’ll only ask for a small, reasonable payment. In other words I’ll not rigidly stick to my normal charges if doing so would be unfair. A lot of my work is repeat business for customers who appreciate my reasonable approach to charging, so you can call me out without worrying about how much it’s going to cost.

Payment

I accept payment in cash or by cheque. Unfortunately I don’t have the facility to process credit or debit card payments. For regular domestic customers, and businesses, I’m happy to operate on a credit basis, where I issue an invoice to be paid within twenty-eight days.

item.title: Reviews
item.description:
What my customers think

Asking for reviews

Once I’ve finished a job for a customer, I often ask if they would be kind enough to write a review. I’m always very grateful to those who have taken the time to do this for me, because even though it doesn’t take very long, customers are often busy. Of course some customers dislike computers so much that they’re too nervous to post a review, so sometimes I do a good job, as usual, but nobody else will ever know. Unfortunately this goes with the territory.

FreeIndex and Google reviews

The following reviews were originally posted on FreeIndex where, on 2nd December 2016, I was rated 46th out of 11,032 people in the ‘UK Computer Service Providers’ category (putting me in the top 0.5 percent and awarding me ‘HIGHLY RECOMMENDED’ status), or on Google:

“I have known John Wallis for many years. He has always responded very promptly – usually the same day – to requests for help with my computer problems, and I have had many! His advice is clearly expressed in language that is appropriate to my level of expertise, and he is happy to retrace his advice if it is apparent that I am still confused! I recommend him without reservations.”

Pat Barker, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 2nd December 2016)

“Contacted on Friday, able to visit promptly the next day. Technical help provided for 2 laptops. Easily sorted, swift service. Great value. Will definitely recommend to friends and seek to use again.”

Ami Tilbrook, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 31st October 2016)

“I would highly recommend the technical services of John Wallis. In fact you would have to be absolutely bonkers not to use the experience of John Wallis which is based upon long work experience with some of the largest industry corporations when like me you needed to replace an entire system, saving all useful data and at a very reasonable cost.”

J. F. Airey, Herringswell (Five-star Google rating awarded 19th October 2016)

“Fast and efficient, pleasant experience and a great price for the amount of work my laptop needed to get it up and running again! I would highly recommend.”

Pern Hollands, Feltwell (Five-star Google rating awarded 3rd September 2016)

“I have known John for several years and I do not hesitate to contact him whenever I have an IT problem. He is easily accessible and very prompt in calling round. He has always resolved the problem quickly and explains it in layman’s terms. I have also always found him very fair and good value for money. I have no hesitation in recommending him.”

Maureen Ford, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 26th July 2016)

“Having used John in the past when he provided an excellent service, I emailed him with a problem I had attaching photos to an email. He replied with a step-by-step guide as to how to solve the problem. Working through John’s guide, I sorted the problem and was able to attach photos within minutes of following his instructions. John is very polite, friendly, professional and extremely knowledgeable and thoroughly deserving of a 5 star recommendation.”

Jenny Porter, Lakenheath (Five-star Google rating awarded 28th June 2016)

“John has helped us on a number of occasions and we have been very pleased with his service. His extensive knowledge is matched by his determination to solve the problem which he has always achieved. We would highly recommend him to you.”

Charles Gentry, Mildenhall (Five-star Google rating awarded 31st March 2016)

“John is a man of integrity. He is extremely knowledgeable about computers and it is clear he keeps up to date with the latest information and advancements of computer software. He fixed the problem easily and efficiently and went beyond the problem to clean my computer of all of the potential harmful downloads, that I did not have a clue about! A lovely gentleman who I would definitely consult in the future for all of my computer needs.”

Theresa Thatcher, Hockwold (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 21st February 2016)

“Excellent service. Understood straight away what was wrong and did not waffle computer terms – explained in plain English what was wrong and fixed the problems straight away – even improve on them – will definitely use again and will recommend to others.”

Sheila Allan, Beck Row (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 16th February 2016)

“I have used John to fix several problems with my desktops. He is always very polite, honest and determined to fix the problem. Always reliable and good value for money. I would highly recommend him.”

Alan Way, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 16th January 2016)

“We’ve had John out a couple of times recently to provide technology assistance and he always works his magic. Whether it is setting up the wireless system after our house move, keeping our old Dell laptop humming along or helping my daughter navigate her new iPad, he does the job. Obviously, his breadth of knowledge is extensive as there seems to be no problem he cannot fix and no job he will not tackle. We are so grateful to have connected with him…thanks again John.”

Gloria Stephens, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 14th January 2016)

“I had a computer that was about fifteen years old and it was running very slowly, so I called John Wallis for help. Unfortunately the computer was obsolete, but John helped me to find a new one running Windows 10 for a very reasonable price and when it arrived he promptly set it up exactly to my requirements. Since then I have had no problems, but whenever I have any questions John is always happy to answer them in an understandable way. If anyone I know ever has any computer problems, I always recommend they get in touch with John.”

Frank Piper, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 12th December 2015)

“John was prompt in answering my plea for help and arrival for the appointed time. After doing all of the usual trouble shooting the root of the problem was not found. He was very persistent and methodical till he finally found the unusual cause of my computer trouble. For a computer that was deemed totally useless, it now performs like new. I am more than delighted with the service and the cost for his call out. Will highly recommend.”

Wanda Bailey, Barton Mills (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 28th November 2015)

“John has sorted out many problems I have had with my laptop over the years. He is extremely knowledgeable, friendly, helpful and always prompt. I would highly recommend John to anyone with computer problems.”

Teresa Brigden, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 7th September 2015)

“ What I spent eight hours on the phone to BT Indian call centres and never got a result John was able to fix in ten minutes. I can’t speak anything but the highest praise.”

Mr. Paul W. Hunt, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 13th May 2015)

“John is extremely knowledgeable and personable. He arrived promptly and was able to remove all the malicious software on my laptop. He is competent and has the ability to explain what he is doing in layman’s terms. I would definitely recommend him.”

Madeleine Storer, West Row (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 22nd March 2015)

“I like to fix my own IT problems but this time it was beyond my skill level. I phoned John in the morning, explaining the problem with my desktop computer. John researched it and visited me the same afternoon. He was able to fix the main problem, an issue with lost restore dates which meant I could not access my profile. He removed unwanted programmes at start up and some other minor issues to help in running the PC quicker. He also got my laptop working again following password issues. He is very knowledgeable, patient and happy to share with you what he is doing and why. I can highly recommend John and would use him again with no hesitation.”

Jenny Porter, Lakenheath (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 1st March 2015)

“Trustworthy. Dependable. Quietly sorts out the computer problem with ease. Very generous with technical information. Patient. Great value for money. Highly recommended individual.”

Jean Berrigan, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 25th February 2015)

“I purchased a new all-in-one PC with Windows 8.1 on it and was unsure how to transfer all my documents and music over from my old Windows XP desktop. I gave John a call and he sounded a nice guy and was very helpful. He arranged to come over and show me how to transfer the files. John helped with the transfer and also showed me a number of things with windows 8.1 as I had never used it before. A few days later I was unsure about the settings for having two users on the computer and sharing files and music and when I spoke to John he had a look into it and popped around a couple of hours later and sorted it out. I would happily recommend John to anyone who needs help with their PC. Very friendly and helpful.”

Mark Thorby, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 13th January 2015)

“John has helped me a couple of times now – once visiting me to help me with my computer and the other giving me advice over the phone. Even though it was a weekend he was there to help. I always keep John’s number handy at home! :).”

Debbie Middleton, Newmarket (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 3rd December 2014)

“We were very pleased with John’s prompt response to our request for help with a problem with our computer and completely satisfied with the efficient way he solved the problem for us.”

Raymond Crane, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 1st December 2014)

“John has been sorting out my computer for a number of years and I have found him excellent and would not hesitate to recommend him.”

Winifred Hudson, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 17th November 2014)

“Excellent work from John with annoying problems that I was experiencing with my computer. John fixed the problems very quickly and also gave me advice about how to get the best use from my computer.”

Mary Withey, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 12th November 2014)

“John Wallis has found solutions for a number of problems I have had with my laptop and Epson printer. He has an extensive knowledge of useful programs available on the Internet. If he cannot sort a problem immediately he keeps on researching until he can. Friendly and prompt service.”

Anne Leakey, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 1st November 2014)

“I would highly recommend John to anyone with any computer problem. He is punctual, efficient and friendly. John has now designed a Web site for my driving school business, advising me and spending time over the design and text. The Web site is now up and running and far superior to the previous site I had. Thank you John.”

Brenda Carey, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 30th October 2014)

“John was able to sort out various problems with my laptop, one being a message for an update of Word 2010, which I thought may have been a virus. My laptop worked very slowly, and now after John has worked his magic it is working much better. I would recommend John to anyone who is having problems with their computer.”

Sandra Bailey, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 24th October 2014)

“I would not hesitate to recommend John to anyone with computer problems. Always punctual, and extremely thorough in his work. Highly recommended.”

D. MacGregor, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 20th October 2014)

“Very prompt in visiting on time. Diagnosed the problem very quickly and solved it on the spot. What a change from so-called ‘experts’ who need to take your PC away! Had a follow-up call to check all was good. I would certainly recommend.”

A. F. Webster, Mildenhall (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 14th October 2014)

“I phoned Mr. Wallis on a Friday evening about downloading printer software from HP for Windows 8 upgraded to 8.1. He promptly came to my home the following morning to help me. He was very kind and professional. I had told him about my problem over the phone the evening before. He researched the problem that I was having and had discovered several different solutions to the problem. He was able to repair the printer issue promptly and thereafter helped me with some minor issues that I did not know how to fix myself. His fees were extremely reasonable. I would highly recommend him for any computer issues. I will call upon him again with any other issues that come up.”

Cindy Williams, Barton Mills (Five-star FreeIndex rating awarded 14th October 2014)

item.title: Setup
item.description:
Setup

Setting up new systems, refreshing existing systems, and decommissioning old systems:

  • removing old hardware and cabling then installing new equipment
  • transferring data from an old system to a new one (programs cannot be easily copied across, so they have to be re-installed)
  • proper disposal of unwanted equipment (you can’t just throw it in the bin because of the Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment Regulations 2013)
  • helping to make the right decisions when using Windows for the first time (such as whether or not to let Microsoft keep a close eye on your activities)
  • securely wiping and physically destroying hard disk drives (which can still contain sensitive data even after you think you’ve obliterated it all)
  • putting the start button back into Windows 8 and 8.1
  • reinstalling operating systems (back to ‘factory settings’)
  • replacing one operating system with another (switching from Windows to Linux will often breathe new life into an old system)
  • installing operating systems side-by-side (you can easily have Windows and Linux installed on the same hardware as a ‘dual-boot’ system)
  • destroying all data on an old system prior to sale or donation
  • installing a new router after a change of Internet service provider

item.title: Terms
item.description:
The way I work

Buying equipment and parts

In the past I’ve bought hardware on behalf of customers and then been stuck with it when they changed their minds about having work done. Therefore I no longer purchase items for customers, but instead just advise them what they need and let them obtain it.

This policy has several practical advantages for customers:

  • Because I’m not spending time ordering items and waiting for them to be delivered, travelling to a shop and buying them in person, or taking the risk of being lumbered with things that I don’t need, I don’t have to pass on any additional costs, and customers end up paying less
  • Customers can be absolutely sure that I’m not making any money out of selling them items at grossly inflated prices (I wouldn’t do that anyway, but there are certainly people in the computer industry who would)
  • If an item fails during its warranty period, it is easier for customers to return it if the paperwork has their details on it rather than mine (many warranties only last as long as the original purchaser owns the item, and if I were to buy the item and sell it on, straight away such a warranty would become useless)

So don’t pay mark-up on parts, just buy what I recommend then only pay for fitting.

Real on-site support

If you ask me a simple question on the telephone then I’ll probably be able to answer it without arranging a visit, but for anything else I’ll usually come to see you in person. I don’t do ‘remote support’ over the Internet for several reasons, not the least of which is the potential security risk of running the necessary software. However, this is not an excuse to charge a call-out fee (see my policy on callout fees).

Free collection and delivery within five miles

If I can’t reasonably fix a problem on-site, I’ll gladly take equipment away to be fixed, and bring it back again, without making a call-out charge for nearby customers. There aren’t many computer support businesses who’ll do that, so this is a good reason to get in touch with me for jobs such as computer repairs and laptop repairs.

Sensitive data

If I need to take away a computer or laptop for repair, I can always remove the hard disk drive first and leave it with you, if it contains sensitive data that you don’t want taken off your premises.

item.title: Troubleshooting
item.description:
Troubleshooting

Fixing misbehaving and non-working computers and laptops. Just about all hardware and software problems tackled, such as:

  • fitting new power supplies to desktop and tower systems
  • repairing hard-to-find desktop and tower power supplies
  • fixing circuit boards with a limited range of failed components
  • replacing broken laptop keyboards
  • removing viruses and other malicious software
  • repairing damaged power leads
  • solving ‘lost date and time’ problems
  • diagnosing ‘Blue Screen of Death’ problems
  • sorting out interference from neighbouring Wi-Fi systems
  • fitting new laptop screens
  • getting ‘Windows Update’ to work properly
  • repairing broken power jacks
  • recovering data from barely readable CDs and DVDs
  • removing passwords from Windows accounts (for versions prior to Windows 8)
  • solving Wi-Fi, broadband, and other connection problems
  • sorting out ‘printer offline’ troubles
  • fixing wireless scanning failures
  • replacing failing hard drives
  • reinstalling malfunctioning software
  • replacing failed graphics cards
  • recovering data from systems that won’t start

item.title: Tuition
item.description:
Tuition

Teaching people how to use computers in many different ways, such as:

  • starting up computers
  • ‘surfing’ the Internet with a browser program
  • zooming browsers in and out
  • setting up a ‘home page’
  • blocking annoying adverts
  • using iPad and Android tablets
  • downloading things from the Internet
  • ‘burning’ (i.e. creating) CDs and DVDs
  • copying files, creating folders, renaming files and folders, etc.
  • buying things online
  • using e-mail
  • dealing with ‘spam’ (unwanted rubbish e-mails)
  • writing letters and other documents
  • creating spreadsheets
  • setting up databases
  • making presentations
  • encrypting office documents
  • transferring photographs from camera to computer
  • viewing Google Maps and StreetView
  • editing photographs
  • filing official forms online
  • security basics
  • simple programming (also known nowadays as ‘coding’)
  • shutting down computers (nicely, and by force if necessary)

item.title: TalkTalk Router Security Advice
item.description:
TalkTalk Router Security Advice

What’s the problem?

Anyone who has a TalkTalk router should consider changing its wireless password following a security breach in which router names and passwords have been taken. If TalkTalk hadn’t kept this information then it couldn’t have been stolen, but they did, and it was, so now it’s up to their customers to take corrective action.

Does it really matter?

People out in the street can usually see the name of your router, and if they know the wireless password as well, they can get on to the Internet using your connection. If you don’t care about the possibility of this happening, then you don’t need to do anything, but if you don’t like the idea of strangers piggy-backing on your service, you need to take action to make sure they can’t get in.

How do I know my router’s details?

There is usually a label stuck on each router giving its name and the password required to connect to it wirelessly. However, you don’t have to stick with the name and password on the label. You can change the name and password that were given to the router when it was made, and make them whatever you like.

Is there any technical terminology?

The router’s name is technically called its Service Set Identifier, so the label will probably say ‘SSID’ rather than ‘name’. Similarly, the label might say ‘wireless key’ rather than ‘wireless password’. While you’re looking at the label, make a note of the administration account name, and the password for that account.

How do I make the changes?

When you run a browser program such as Internet Explorer, Firefox, Chrome, or Safari, you would normally use it to look at various sites on the Web. However, you can use a browser to talk directly to the router, rather than to the outside world. The router is actually a computer in its own right, and you can log on to it to change its numerous settings, including its name and wireless password.

What’s the exact procedure?

Before you start, please note that although this is not a complex or risky procedure, I do not accept any responsibility if you decide to use it to change the wireless password of your router.

  1. Start up your favourite browser program for surfing the Internet.
  2. In the bar at the top where you would normally type something like ‘www.bbc.co.uk’, type ‘192.168.0.1’ instead (this is the router’s own address).
  3. The next bit depends on the exact model of your router, but you should be invited to log in with a user name and password (please note that the user name to use is not the router’s name, and the password to use is not the wireless password).
  4. The user name for logging in to the router is normally ‘admin’ (it could be something else, though I have never seen any other user name used for router administration). However, if there is another administration account name on the label stuck to the router, use that instead.
  5. The password for logging in to the router as an administrator is quite often just ‘admin’ again (yes I know, the security on some routers is a joke) though on a modern router there should be a more secure administration password on the label stuck to the router.
  6. Once you have logged in, you will see many different options, but the one that you are interested in will be the wireless settings.
  7. Once you have found the screen showing the wireless password, change it. Usually you have to type a new password in the ‘wireless password’ or ‘wireless key’ box, then click an ‘Apply’ button. Please be very slow and careful doing this, because there is often no ‘type it again’ box to make sure that you have really entered what you thought you had entered.
  8. Note that if you are using a wireless connection to change the wireless password, your connection will be lost as soon as you have made the password change. It will then be necessary to re-make the connection to your router, typing in the new wireless password to do so.
  9. Also remember that all other wireless devices in your home will need to re-connect to the router using the new wireless password. This procedure might seem like a lot of trouble, but at least you will know that nobody out in the street who has managed to obtain your old wireless password will be able to use your Internet connection any more.
